content.problemStatement:
You are given an undirected graph G=(V,E)G = (V, E)G=(V,E) containing NNN nodes and MMM edges. The nodes are numbered from 1 to NNN. A subset CCC of VVV is a vertex cover if for every edge (u,v)∈E(u, v) \in E(u,v)∈E, at least one of uuu and vvv belong to CCC. Note that C=VC = VC=V is always a vertex cover.

Consider a partition of VVV into two sets AAA and BBB. It is said to be a valid partition if the following two conditions are satisfied: AAA should be a vertex cover. And for each iii such that 1≤i≤n/21 \leq i \leq n/21≤i≤n/2, nodes 2i2i2i and 2i−12i - 12i−1 don't belong to the same set (i.e., one belongs to set AAA and the other to set BBB).

Determine if a valid partition exists. If it exists, provide an example of one valid partition.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM denoting the number of nodes and number of edges in the graph respectively.
Each of the following MMM lines contains two space-separated integers uuu and vvv denoting an edge between nodes uuu and vvv.


Output Format

For each test case, print a line containing the string `"possible"` (without quotes) if a solution exists or `"impossible"` otherwise.
If a solution exists, print a second line containing a binary string. The iii-th character of this string should be `'0'` if vertex iii is in set BBB or `'1'` if it is in set AAA.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases <= 10^6
Sum of M over all test cases <= 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two space-separated integers N and M, denoting the number of nodes and the number of edges in the graph, respectively. Following this, there are M lines, each containing two space-separated integers u and v, denoting an edge between nodes u and v.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a line containing the string 'possible' if a valid partition exists, or 'impossible' otherwise. If a solution exists, print a second line containing a binary string where the i-th character is '0' if vertex i is in set B or '1' if it is in set A.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: We can put nodes numbered 2 and 3 in set A and node 1 in set B. Note that this is a valid partition because set A is a vertex cover; also, nodes numbered 1 and 2 belong to different sets.
Example case 2: There exists no partition which satisfies the conditions.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a graph traversal approach. We start by initializing two sets, A and B, to empty sets. Then, we iterate over each edge in the graph. For each edge (u, v), we check if u is in set A and v is in set B, or vice versa. If neither condition is met, we add u to set A and v to set B. We also keep track of the nodes that have been assigned to set A or set B. After iterating over all edges, we check if the partition is valid by verifying that each edge has at least one node in set A and that no two adjacent nodes are in the same set.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * (N + M)) — where T is the number of test cases, N is the number of nodes, and M is the number of edges. The reason is that we iterate over each edge in the graph for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + M) — where N is the number of nodes and M is the number of edges. The reason is that we need to store the nodes and edges in the graph.
